era_context: The Han Dynasty is in terminal decline; the Yellow Turban Rebellion (184–205 CE) has shattered central authority. Regional warlords control vast territories nominally under the Han court. The period of the Three Kingdoms (220–280 CE) is beginning. In the Mediterranean, the Roman Empire under Elagabalus faces internal instability. In South Asia, the Satavahana Empire is fragmenting. In China, the formal partition into three competing states is imminent.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record for 220 CE specifically)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for this exact year)

events.title: Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei; formal end of Han Dynasty authority in north
events.description: Cao Cao, the dominant warlord of northern China, is formally proclaimed King of Wei by the Han court—a title that effectively acknowledges his independent sovereignty while maintaining nominal Han suzerainty. This marks the practical end of centralized Han authority in the north and accelerates the fragmentation into three competing kingdoms. Cao Cao's son Cao Pi will formally depose the Han emperor in 220 CE (or 221 CE depending on source), establishing the Wei Dynasty. This event crystallizes decades of warlord competition following the Yellow Turban Rebellion.
key_figures: Cao Cao (King of Wei, military commander)

sources.name: Records of Wei (Wei Lüe) and Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The date 220 CE is conventional for Cao Cao's kingship; some sources place the formal proclamation in 216 CE. The deposition of the Han emperor by Cao Pi occurred in 220 or 221 CE depending on the source tradition. Dating is secure to within 1–2 years.
cross_references: Leads to 221-001 (Cao Pi founds Wei Dynasty)

events.title: Liu Bei establishes base in Yizhou (Sichuan); consolidates southwestern territory
events.description: Liu Bei, a warlord claiming descent from the Han imperial house, consolidates control over Yizhou (modern Sichuan province) after years of military campaigns. With the strategist Zhuge Liang as his advisor, Liu Bei establishes a stable territorial base that will become the foundation of the Later Han (Shu) state. This consolidation is crucial to the three-way balance of power that defines the subsequent Three Kingdoms period. Yizhou's agricultural wealth and defensible terrain make it a viable independent power base.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)
Zhuge Liang (strategist, Prime Minister of Shu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE, but based on earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou occurred over several years (c. 214–220 CE). The year 220 CE is conventional for the completion of this process. Some sources date the final conquest of Liu Zhang to 214 CE.
cross_references: Leads to 221-002 (Liu Bei proclaims himself emperor of Shu)

events.title: Sun Ce's successor Sun Quan consolidates control of southern territories (Jiangdong)
events.description: Sun Quan, who inherited control of the Jiangdong region from his father Sun Ce and brother Sun Strategy, consolidates his authority over the wealthy southern territories. By 220 CE, Sun Quan has secured his position against internal rivals and external threats, establishing the territorial base that will become the Eastern Wu state. The southern region's economic resources and geographic isolation from northern warlords give Sun Quan strategic advantages. This consolidation completes the three-way territorial division that characterizes the Three Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Sun Quan (warlord, later emperor of Eastern Wu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Sun Quan's consolidation was gradual, occurring over the period 200–220 CE. By 220 CE, his authority was secure. The exact year is conventional rather than precisely documented.
cross_references: Leads to 222-001 (Sun Quan proclaimed King of Wu)

events.title: Elagabalus becomes Roman Emperor; religious and political upheaval
events.region: Roman Empire (centered on Rome and Syria)
events.description: Elagabalus (Varius Avitus Bassianus), a Syrian priest of the sun god Elagabal, becomes Roman Emperor at approximately age 14, following the assassination of Macrinus. His reign is marked by religious innovation (attempting to elevate the cult of Elagabal above traditional Roman religion), sexual scandal (by Roman standards), and political instability. The Severan dynasty is fragmenting. Elagabalus's reign lasts only four years (220–222 CE) and is characterized by rapid turnover of officials, military discontent, and factional conflict. His reign illustrates the instability of the third-century Roman Empire.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE
Cassius Dio, Roman History (written early 3rd century CE, covers this period)

events.certainty_note: Elagabalus's accession date is well-attested in Roman sources, though the Historia Augusta is notoriously unreliable for details. The year 220 CE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to 222-002 (Elagabalus assassinated; Severus Alexander becomes emperor)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Ardashir I consolidates power; Parthian Empire in decline
events.description: Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid Persian Empire, continues consolidating power in Persia and Mesopotamia, effectively ending Parthian Arsacid rule. By 220 CE, Ardashir has defeated the last Parthian king, Artabanus V, and established the Sassanid dynasty as the dominant power in the Near East. This marks a major geopolitical shift: the Sassanids will be Rome's primary rival for the next four centuries. Ardashir's reign emphasizes Zoroastrianism, centralized administration, and military reorganization.
key_figures: Artabanus V (last Parthian king, defeated c. 224 CE)

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE, based on earlier traditions)
Cassius Dio and later Roman historians (accounts of Sassanid rise)

events.certainty_note: Ardashir's rise occurred over the period 208–224 CE. The exact year of his consolidation is uncertain; 220 CE is approximate. The final defeat of Artabanus V is dated to c. 224 CE by most sources.
cross_references: Leads to 224-001 (Ardashir defeats Artabanus V; Sassanid Empire formally established)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of Cao Cao's kingship (220 CE) is disputed by some scholars, who place it in 216 CE. The exact year of Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou is debated; some sources favor 214 CE. Elagabalus's religious policies are often exaggerated in the Historia Augusta; modern scholarship suggests his reign was less scandalous than portrayed, though still unstable. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is traditionally dated to 224 CE, not 220 CE; the year 220 CE represents the consolidation phase rather than the final military victory.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 220 CE is heavily weighted toward China and the Roman Empire, where written sources are abundant. Chinese sources (Sanguozhi, later compilations) are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. Roman sources are similarly late and often unreliable (Historia Augusta). South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent from standard historiography. The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese sources, but the exact dating of events within the 220–222 CE transition is sometimes uncertain. Western bias in surviving sources is significant.

graph_edges.note: Cao Cao's kingship directly led to his son Cao Pi's deposition of the Han emperor and founding of Wei Dynasty
Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou enabled his proclamation as emperor of Shu in 221 CE
Sun Quan's consolidation led to his formal proclamation as King of Wu
Elagabalus's unstable reign led to his assassination and replacement by Severus Alexander
Cao Cao's northern consolidation and Liu Bei's southwestern consolidation occurred simultaneously, defining the three-way balance
Roman instability under Elagabalus coincided with Sassanid consolidation, shifting Near Eastern balance of power
